whoUses: young people, especially in São Paulo

natural: to embarrass oneself / make a fool of yourself

examples.text: Esqueci o nome dela na frente de todo mundo, paguei o maior mico.
examples.translation: I forgot her name in front of everyone — so embarrassing.

whoUses: everyone under retirement age, nationwide; the default casual thank-you
usageContext: casual thanks, and often a sign-off when leaving or ending a call ('valeu, falou!')

examples.text: Tenho que ir. Valeu, até amanhã!
examples.translation: I have to go. Cheers, see you tomorrow!

usageContext: two uses: as a noun for 'guy' ('esse cara' = 'that guy') and as a vocative 'dude' at the start of a sentence ('Cara, você não vai acreditar…'). Note 'cara' is grammatically feminine ('o cara' for the person, but 'a cara' = the face)

whoUses: strongly associated with the Northeast (Recife, Salvador, Fortaleza), all ages; understood everywhere
usageContext: praising anything — a party, a song, a plan ('que massa!'); saying it marks you as NE-flavored, which lands warmly

examples.text: O show ontem foi massa demais!
examples.translation: The show yesterday was really awesome!

whoUses: paulistanos of all ages; a São Paulo shibboleth the way 'maneiro' is for Rio
usageContext: praising things or people ('um rolê da hora', 'ele é da hora' = he's a good guy)

examples.text: Achei um bar da hora perto da Paulista.
examples.translation: I found a really cool bar near Paulista Avenue.

whoUses: cariocas of all ages; instantly marks Rio de Janeiro speech, though understood nationwide
usageContext: praising anything ('que maneiro!'); agrees in gender: 'uma praia maneira'

examples.text: Esse filme é maneiro demais, você tem que ver.
examples.translation: This movie is so cool, you have to see it.

literal: a spin / a roll (from 'rolar', to roll)
natural: an outing / a hang / going out with friends
whoUses: teens through thirty-somethings nationwide; core youth vocabulary, also spelled 'rolé'
usageContext: making plans: 'dar um rolê' = to go out / cruise around; 'bora pro rolê?' = coming out tonight?; 'rolê aleatório' = a random adventure (internet-famous phrase)

examples.text: Vamos dar um rolê na praia depois do almoço?
examples.translation: Want to go hang out at the beach after lunch?

examples.text: O rolê de ontem foi da hora.

whoUses: everyone in informal speech, nationwide, all ages
usageContext: any casual talk about money: 'tô sem grana' = I'm broke; 'uma grana boa' = good money. Fine with friends and coworkers you're casual with; use 'dinheiro' in formal or business writing

examples.text: Não vou sair hoje, tô sem grana até sexta.
examples.translation: I'm not going out today, I'm out of cash until Friday.

whoUses: literally everyone, every region, every age — one of the most frequent words in spoken Brazilian Portuguese
usageContext: tacked onto the end of statements to seek agreement ('Tá calor, né?') or sprinkled mid-sentence as a filler; also a standalone reply meaning 'right?!'. Fine in speech at any informality level; avoid only in formal writing

examples.text: Esse trânsito de São Paulo é impossível, né?
examples.translation: This São Paulo traffic is impossible, right?

examples.text: — Ele chegou atrasado de novo. — Né?!
examples.translation: — He showed up late again. — Right?!

natural: to hook up with someone — make out, casually, with no commitment implied
whoUses: teens and adults in dating contexts, nationwide; the standard word for pre-relationship romance
usageContext: parties, dating apps, gossip: 'ficamos ontem' = we made out last night. 'Ficar' usually means kissing, not necessarily sex — context decides. The stages are: ficar → 'ficante' (casual thing) → namorar (official relationship). Asking 'quer ficar comigo?' is a direct come-on
warningNotes: Not rude, but unambiguously romantic/physical — don't confuse it with plain 'ficar' (to stay). Saying 'fiquei com ela' announces you made out with her, so mind who you say it to.

examples.text: Eles ficaram na festa, mas não estão namorando.
examples.translation: They hooked up at the party, but they're not dating.

examples.text: Ela é minha ficante, nada sério ainda.
examples.translation: She's my casual thing, nothing serious yet.
